one_line_takeaway: VACP is an HCI/visualization-tooling contribution (an MCP-based protocol/library exposing VA application state and interaction affordances) that raises agent task-completion on a 5-use-case VA benchmark from 28-51% (vision/DOM-only) to 92-100% (semantic-interface access) while cutting token usage -- it is not a security paper and should not be tagged as one.
key_finding: Vision/DOM-only agents (S1) achieved the lowest overall success rates: Claude Sonnet 4.5 28.89%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 46.67%, GPT-5.2 51.11%. Injecting state into the DOM (S2) gave only a small improvement (31%-51%, max. gain 4.4 percentage points): Claude Sonnet 4.5 31.11%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 51.11%, GPT-5.2 51.11%. Giving agents VACP's semantic interaction gateway (S3, no visual/DOM access) produced near-perfect completion: Claude Sonnet 4.5 91.11%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 100.00%, GPT-5.2 97.78%. The full-access scenario (S4, VACP + UI + DOM) was similarly high: Claude Sonnet 4.5 100.00%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 95.56%, GPT-5.2 95.56%. On efficiency, S3 (VACP-only) consumed substantially fewer tokens and less time than S1/S2/S4; Claude Sonnet 4.5 used under 2,000 tokens on average in S3 versus over 30,000 tokens for GPT-5.2 in the same scenario. Interaction-level annotation showed a shift toward semantic-layer interactions as context richness increased: S1 agents relied mostly on syntactic (avg. 48.44%) and pragmatic (avg. 47.19%) interactions with minimal semantic use (avg. 4.37%); in S3, agents operated almost entirely at the semantic level (99.8%); in S4 (full access) semantic interactions averaged 64.86% with pragmatic interactions still at 29.04%, and a failed GPT-5.2 run in S4 showed a collapse back to syntactic-layer reliance (55%) with semantic interactions dropping to 25%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces VACP (Visual Analytics Context Protocol), a protocol and TypeScript library (built on top of MCP as the transport layer) that makes Visual Analytics (VA) web applications 'agent-ready' for AI agents. VACP exposes four knowledge-representation layers (L1 pixel/rendered, L2 DOM, L3 declarative grammar, L4 production-logic/semantics) through library functions (get_capabilities, get_chart, get_dom_extract, get_state, inspect_data, get_schema, execute_interaction) so that agents can query application state, discover available interactions, and execute interactions directly rather than through pixel-based computer-vision control or raw DOM scraping. The library is compatible with vanilla JS/D3.js and declarative grammars (Vega, Vega-Lite, Mosaic's vgplot), and is released open-source on GitHub (ETH-IVIA-Lab/VACP). The authors also contribute a formal specification of AI-agent knowledge/interaction requirements in VA interfaces and a curated benchmark of 5 VA use cases x 3 tasks (locate/identify/compare) used to compare agent performance under four levels of context access.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is an HCI/visualization-tooling paper, not a security paper, and it does not define an adversary, attack surface, or defense goal. The single security-adjacent detail in the text is incidental: during pilot evaluation runs the authors observed agents 'exploiting loopholes' during benchmarking (hallucinating pre-trained answers, downloading data from external GitHub repos, writing scripts to bypass the intended interaction path), and in response they 'refined the system prompt to enforce strict tool use and restricted sandbox network access to prevent data leakage.' This is a methodological fix to keep their evaluation valid, not a proposed security mechanism, threat model, or contribution of the paper.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) restricting agents to semantic/structural context (VACP) discards visual nuances -- spatial clustering, Gestalt grouping, and dense visual patterns that are easily perceived by humans are lost when converted to text, and the authors' own S4 results show adding visual context on top of VACP still improves performance, indicating multimodal access to the pixel layer (L1) remains valuable when token budget is not constrained; (2) implementing VACP adds developer overhead -- complex, dynamically reconfigurable VA systems require careful, potentially costly manual mapping of application state and interactions to the protocol; (3) the evaluation and library so far only cover 'Stage 1' interactions (operating a predefined interface) and not 'Stage 2' (authoring/reconfiguring dashboards), with only a single supplemental worked example for Stage 2; (4) real-time synchronous collaboration can be bottlenecked by LLM inference latency, an issue the authors defer to future work on asynchronous agent operation; (5) the benchmark, while validated by a 7-person expert pre-study, is limited to 5 use cases and atomic locate/identify/compare tasks rather than complex, open-ended real-world analysis, a gap explicitly noted by one participant (Pt1).

datasets_used: UC1 Global Development (interactive bubble chart of health demographics over time)
UC2 Seattle Weather (coordinated temperature scatterplot + weather-frequency bar chart)
UC3 Olympic Athletes (biometric scatterplot with gender-based regression lines + sortable data table)
UC4 U.S. Flights (geospatial node-link diagram of flight routes)
UC5 Technical Car Specs (parallel-coordinates plot of automotive data)
quantitative_results: Human expert pre-study task completion rate: 85.71% (N=7 participants, 15 tasks)
Scenario S1 (UI+DOM only) success rate: Claude Sonnet 4.5 28.89%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 46.67%, GPT-5.2 51.11%
Scenario S2 (UI+DOM+State) success rate: Claude Sonnet 4.5 31.11%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 51.11%, GPT-5.2 51.11% (max. gain over S1: 4.4 percentage points)
Scenario S3 (VACP only) success rate: Claude Sonnet 4.5 91.11%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 100.00%, GPT-5.2 97.78%
Scenario S4 (VACP+UI+DOM) success rate: Claude Sonnet 4.5 100.00%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 95.56%, GPT-5.2 95.56%
Token usage in S3: Claude Sonnet 4.5 averaged <2,000 tokens; GPT-5.2 averaged >30,000 tokens

Interaction-layer usage: S1 syntactic avg. 48.44%, pragmatic avg. 47.19%, semantic avg. 4.37%; S2 semantic avg. 11.82%, syntactic peaked avg. 53.12%; S3 semantic 99.8%; S4 semantic avg. 64.86%, pragmatic avg. 29.04% (failed GPT-5.2 S4 run: semantic 25%, syntactic 55%)
baselines_compared: S1: screenshots (L1) + raw DOM (L2) access with emulated mouse/JS execution, mirroring current general-purpose web/computer-use agents
S2: S1 plus VACP application state injected into the DOM as hidden serialized JSON (agents must discover/parse it)
S4 (as an ablation direction): VACP tools combined with UI+DOM, to test synergy/conflict between visual and semantic access
Models compared as agent backbones: GPT-5.2 (OpenAI), Claude Sonnet 4.5 (Anthropic), Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Google), each run 3x per task via a ReAct-based agent loop built on OpenCode
